coredata.dc:title: Assessment of the therapeutic potential of probiotics against carbon quantum dots-induced neurotoxicity in common carp (Cyprinus carpio)
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Xianglin
Yuan, Rongjie
Sun, Dandan
Ji, Xinyu
Wei, Yinyin
Li, Lulu
Guo, Suqi
Li, Baohua
Chen, Jianjun
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have received increasing attention in recent years for their potential toxicity. However, little is known about their neurobehavioral toxicity. This study aimed to investigate the potential mechanisms by which probiotics reduce CQDs neurotoxicity from a brain-gut axis perspective by exposing carp to CQDs and/or probiotics for five weeks. The results showed that CQDs accumulation in the brain reduces the expression of blood-brain-barrier (BBB) related genes in carp, leading to brain damage. In addition, CQDs impaired motor behavior and inhibited acetylcholinesterase activity. These abnormalities were alleviated by probiotic supplementation. Microbiomic analysis showed that probiotics improved the imbalance of intestinal flora caused by CQDs and increased the abundance of Firmicutes. Serum metabolomic analysis showed that probiotic supplementation restored the abnormal metabolic levels associated with neurological, inflammatory, and apoptotic cell death caused by CQDs. Overall, probiotic supplementation improved the CQDs-induced changes in brain damage, gut microbiology, and systemic metabolism. These results suggests that CQDs may cause neurotoxicity via the brain-gut microbial axis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microbiome
Metabolome
Blood brain barrier
Neuroprotective effects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Extraction of RNA, reverse transcription real-time fluorescence quantitative polymerase chain reaction (PCR)
Expression of autophagy-, inflammation-, immunity-, tight junction-, and oxidative stress-related genes in the brain
Diversity and composition of gut microbiota
Correlation analysis of differential metabolites with apoptosis, oxidative stress, inflammation, and behavior
Spearman correlation analysis of intestinal microorganisms and serum metabolites

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology of Henan Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Henan Province Science and Technology Development Plan Project in China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Henan Province Science and Technology Development Plan Project in China (grant number 212102110359, 222102110362).
This work was supported by the Innovation Project of Henan Normal University (YL202004).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption and accumulation of CQDs in brain tissue of each experimental group (scale bar = 500 μm).
Effect of CQDs exposure and probiotic treatment on brain tissue morphology [e.g., microglia nuclear fixation (red arrows) and neuronal degenerative atrophy (black arrows)] (scale bar = 50 μm).

Effect of CQDs exposure and probiotic recovery on AChE activity and oxidative stress (MDA, SOD, GSH-Px). The values represent the mean ± SD. Different letters indicate significant differences (P<0.05, Tukey's post hoc test).

Changes in oxidative stress, tight junctions, apoptosis, autophagy, and inflammatory responses after five weeks of CQDs exposure in carp. (A) mRNA levels of autophagy- and apoptosis-related genes (atg5, lc3Ⅱ, caspase-3, caspase-9). (B) mRNA levels of tight junction-related genes (ocludin, zo-1, claudin-2). (C) mRNA levels of inflammation-related genes (il-1β, tnf-α, il-10, nrf2, ho-1). Values represent the mean ± SD. The mRNA expression levels values are normalized to 40S and expressed as a ratio to the control. Different letters indicate significant differences (P<0.05, Tukey's post hoc test).

Effect of CQDs exposure and probiotic recovery on locomotor behavior. (A) Movement trajectory of fish in the test tank. (B) Average swimming speed. (C) Average acceleration. (D) Total distance moved by larvae. The values represent the mean ± SD. Different letters indicate significant differences (P<0.05, Tukey's post hoc test).

Comparison of α-diversity and β-diversity of the gut microbiota and flora composition among the groups. (A) Microbiota richness (Chao1 index). (B) Microbiota diversity (Shannon index). (C) Dilution curves. (D) Unweighted UniFrac PCoA of carp gut microbiota. (E) Community composition at the phylum level. Asterisks indicate significant differences (*P< 0.05, **P<0.01). PCoA, principal coordinate analysis.

Analysis of differential metabolite content and correlation of differential metabolites with other indicators. (A) Violin plots of some of the differential metabolites among the groups. (B) Spearman correlation analysis of differential metabolites with behavioral, apoptotic, inflammatory, immune, and oxidative stress-related indicators. Asterisks indicate significant differences (*P<0.05, **P<0.01).
Spearman's correlation analysis of intestinal microorganisms and serum metabolites.

head.ce:title: Assessment of the therapeutic potential of probiotics against carbon quantum dots-induced neurotoxicity in common carp (Cyprinus carpio)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Fisheries, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, 453007, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCollege of Fisheries, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, 4530enan N07, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang, 453007, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bCollege of Life Science, Henan Normal University, Xinxiang 453007, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have received increasing attention in recent years for their potential toxicity. However, little is known about their neurobehavioral toxicity. This study aimed to investigate the potential mechanisms by which probiotics reduce CQDs neurotoxicity from a brain-gut axis perspective by exposing carp to CQDs and/or probiotics for five weeks. The results showed that CQDs accumulation in the brain reduces the expression of blood-brain-barrier (BBB) related genes in carp, leading to brain damage. In addition, CQDs impaired motor behavior and inhibited acetylcholinesterase activity. These abnormalities were alleviated by probiotic supplementation. Microbiomic analysis showed that probiotics improved the imbalance of intestinal flora caused by CQDs and increased the abundance of Firmicutes. Serum metabolomic analysis showed that probiotic supplementation restored the abnormal metabolic levels associated with neurological, inflammatory, and apoptotic cell death caused by CQDs. Overall, probiotic supplementation improved the CQDs-induced changes in brain damage, gut microbiology, and systemic metabolism. These results suggests that CQDs may cause neurotoxicity via the brain-gut microbial axis.

ce:keyword.ce:text: orthogonal partial least squares discriminant analysis

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new nanomaterial widely used in recent years, has excellent hydrophilicity, simple synthesis, environmental friendliness, low cost, and low toxicity. They have been widely used in biomedicine, sewage treatment, and other applications (Kumar et al., 2022), consisting of spherical nanoparticles in discrete states with diameters less than 10 nm (Azam et al., 2021). Moreover, CQDs are characterized by high water solubility and biocompatibility due to the presence of numerous carboxyl and carbonyl groups on their surface (Kumar et al., 2022). However, given their widespread use and high-water solubility, the potential biosafety implications of CQDs, particularly their impact on aquatic organisms, warrants further investigation. Quantum dots have been found to cross the blood-brain-barrier (BBB) and accumulate in the brain (Song et al., 2019). Nanoparticles also carry a risk of bioaccumulation in various organs and tissues, potentially burdening to the body (Wu et al., 2016). Moreover, in vitro and rodent experiments have demonstrated that nanoparticles can impair dopaminergic and serotonergic systems, alter neuronal morphology, lead to autophagy and neuronal death, and trigger neurodegenerative changes (Zhang et al., 2014). Furthermore, exposure to quantum dots has been shown to decrease the activity of superoxide dismutase and the levels of glutathione in neurons and tissues, increase intracellular reactive oxygen species production, elevate inflammation levels increases, and induce behavioral changes (Wu et al., 2016). These findings indicate that quantum dots can trigger oxidative stress in the nervous system, including the brain, resulting in a series of neurotoxic effects (Gao et al., 2013). The quantum dots that have been investigated for their neurotoxicity include CdSe, ZnS, CdTe, and CdS (Wu et al., 2016).

Probiotics consist of bacteria or yeast and can be considered functional foods that can recolonize and restore the symbiosis of the intestinal flora. The probiotics including Lactobacillus acidophilus, Bacillus subtilis, and Phaffia rhodozyma are beneficial to their hosts (Chen et al., 2022c). Specifically, B. subtilis delays neurodegeneration and cognitive impairment in Cryptobacterium hidradenum (Cogliati et al., 2020); L. acidophilus has shown promise in improving not only the intestinal environment but also in addressing neurological disorders such as depression, anxiety, and cognitive impairment (Wu et al., 2019). Furthermore, L. acidophilus can ameliorate brain damage through the brain-gut axis and act as a neuroprotective agent (Ma et al., 2019). Finally, P. rhodozyma has been used to promote growth performance, antioxidant activity, meat quality, and anti-inflammatory responses in animals (Chen et al., 2022c). The intestinal barrier is essential for maintaining homeostasis of the intestinal microbiota. Previous studies have demonstrated that exposure to CQDs can cause damage to the intestinal barrier and disorders of the intestinal flora (Chen et al., 2022b). However, it remains unclear whether the probiotic composition of these three bacteria can mitigate the potential neurotoxicity induced by CQDs.

The gut microbiota has been shown to reduce stress-induced behaviors in humans and rodents by modulating the microbiota-gut-brain axis and the microbiota-gut-immune axis to ameliorate inflammatory responses (Yano et al., 2015). In addition, the therapeutic effects of probiotics on neurological disorders have been found to depend largely on the modulation of gut microbes and metabolites (Li et al., 2022). For example, L. acidophilus can improve neurodegeneration by increasing butyric acid levels (Srivastav et al., 2019), whereas, B. subtilis can influence host metabolism, particularly inflammation and insulin resistance, by modulating gut microbiome composition and metabolites, thereby preventing neuronal cell death and brain dysfunction (Zhang et al., 2021).

CQDs can cause intestinal flora disorders and reduce microbial diversity (Chen et al., 2022b). Dysbiosis of the intestinal microbiota can cause damage to the intestinal barrier, facilitates bacterial translocation, and produces lipopolysaccharide (LPS), which can trigger systemic inflammatory responses via the blood circulation system (Ma et al., 2019). Research shows that intestinal microbiota can reduce inflammatory reactions by regulating the gut-brain axis and gut-microbiota-immune axis, thereby reducing stress-induced behavior in humans and rodents (Yano et al., 2015). It is possible that the bacterial metabolites can influence the brain through systemic transmission, thereby modulating the levels of relevant metabolites in the brain and ultimately impacting brain function and cognition (Chen et al., 2021). Probiotics have been extensively used to reduce oxidative stress and inflammation, and alleviate damage (Lin et al., 2022). They have been shown to exert a therapeutic effect on neurological diseases, largely through their ability to regulate intestinal microorganisms and metabolites (Li et al., 2022).

In this study, we investigated whether probiotics could ameliorative brain damage induced by exposure to CQDs, further affecting the behavior of carp. In addition, recovery from a brain injury was assessed by observing histological patterns and detecting gene expression of immune-, apoptotic-, autophagy-, and tight junction-related factors and antioxidant capacity. We employed microbiomics and metabolomics approaches to assess the toxicological relevance of metabolites and investigate the correlations between gut microbes and serum metabolites, to elucidate the potential mechanisms underlying the neuroprotective effects of probiotics against CQDs-induced neurotoxicity.

In this study, the CQDs solution was obtained from Xingzi Material Technology Development Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The product used was WB11146–50 mg, with a concentration of 20 mg/ml. TEM analysis showed that CQDs were spherical with a diameter of approximately 3.1 nm, and the maximum emission wavelength was approximately 460 nm. Further details on the morphology and maximum absorption peak of the CQDs can be found in our previously published article (Chen et al., 2022b). The probiotics used in this study were a combination of L. acidophilus, B. subtilis, and P. rhodozyma, which were prepared using the same manner as previous studies in our experiments (Chen et al., 2022c).

The probiotics were sprayed evenly into the feed at 2% of the base feed and placed in a refrigerator at 4 °C for backup. During the feeding experiment, the viability of probiotics was tested weekly to ensure their effectiveness.

Test fish were bought from a local fishery in Xinxiang City. A total of 300 juvenile carp (Cyprinus carpio) with an initial mean weight of 28.80 ± 0.59 g were divided into 12 polyethylene tanks with a capacity of 300 L. The commercial diet was fed at a 2% feeding rate for two weeks to acclimatize them to the environment before starting the experiment. During the experiment, all juvenile fish were housed in ventilated animal houses (maintained on a 12-hour light/dark cycle), and the water temperature was maintained at 24 ± 2 °C.

The fish were randomly divided into four groups: 1) control (CON, 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution [PBS]), 2) probiotic (PRO), 3) CQDs (20 mg/kg BW of CQDs), and 4) probiotic recovery (CQDs-PRO) groups. After diluting the CQDs with PBS, each fish in the CQDs and CQDs-PRO groups was orally administered 200 μL of CQDs solution using a gavage. The fish in the CON and PRO groups were given the same volume of PBS. The oral gavage lasted for 5 weeks, during which time the fish were simultaneously fed diets with or without probiotics twice daily.

Each tank contained 25 fish, and there were 3 replicate tanks per experimental group. After for a 5-week feeding period, the fish were sampled after fasting for approximately 12 h. The fish were removed from each tank and anesthetized with MS-222. Six fish were randomly selected from each tank for intravenous blood collection for metabolomic assay. Additionally, three fish from each tank had their brain tissues taken and soaked in 4% paraformaldehyde for histological analysis. Eight fish were taken from each tank for intestinal microbiota analysis and put into lyophilization tubes, snap-frozen in liquid nitrogen, and stored at −80 °C. Another eight fish were removed from each tank for behavioral testing, and their brain tissues were taken into small tubes at the end of behavioral testing, snap-frozen in liquid nitrogen, and stored at –80 °C for enzyme activity assay and total RNA extraction.

All animal procedures were conducted in accordance with the guidelines for the care and use of laboratory animals and approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of Henan Normal University (Approval ID: HNSD-SMKX-2120BS0716).

At week five, brain tissue from the fish was collected and put in 4% paraformaldehyde for 12 h. The sections were sliced to thickness using a rotary sectioning machine (Leica PM2245; Germany), followed by staining with hematoxylin and eosin (HE), and observed under a general light microscope (Nikon Eclipse E400; Japan) at the end of filming. The determination of CQDs (fluorescent blue) distribution in the brain tissue was achieved using a fluorescence microscope (Zeiss Axio Vert. A1; Germany). Quantification of fluorescence intensity in brain tissue sections and counting of microglia numbers using ImageJ software using ImageJ software (version 1.51; NIH, Bethesda, Maryland, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of RNA, reverse transcription real-time fluorescence quantitative polymerase chain reaction (PCR)
ce:section.ce:para: Total RNA was extracted from the brain using a TRIzol reagent. The quantity and quality of the RNA were assessed using 1% agarose gel electrophoresis and spectrophotometry at 260 and 280 nm. Total RNA was synthesized into cDNA using a TaKaRa PrimeScript Reverse Transcription Kit. All real-time PCR analyses were performed using the Light96 real-time PCR detection system. Amplification was performed in a 96-well plate containing 5 μL SYBR PrimeScript, 0.2 μL forward primer and reverse primers, 0.5 μL template cDNA, and 4.1 μL ribonuclease-free water in a 10 μL reaction volume. Real-time quantitative PCR amplification was performed for each sample using the following conditions: pre-denaturation at 95 °C for two min, denaturation at 95 °C, annealing at 60 °C for 30s, and extension at 72 °C. All primers involved in this study were designed using Primer Premier 5, and the designed primer sequences were subsequently sent to Beijing Genomics Institution for synthesis. The primer sequence is described in Supplementary Table S1. We calculated the relative transcripts of the target genes with selected reference gene transcripts (40s RNA) using the 2−ΔΔCT method.

We utilized previously research methods from our laboratory to explore the behavioral activities of fish (Chen et al., 2022a). In brief, a camera was mounted in the same position above each tank to ensure a consistent field of view. The behavior of the fish in each experimental group was recorded on video and monitored. Eight fish were randomly selected from each tank for testing, with only one fish released in the tank for each test. After waiting for 10 min, the movement trajectory was recorded for the next three minutes. The real-time tracking systems UMATracker (Yamanaka and Takeuchi, 2018) and ToxTrac (Rodriguez et al., 2018) were utilized to track the position of all fish in the videos, allowing for the calculation of total swimming distance (mm), mean swimming speed (mm/s), mean acceleration (mm/s2), and exploration rate.

Brain tissue was washed twice with 0.9% normal saline, homogenized on ice with 0.9% normal saline, and centrifuged at 4 °C for 10 min, and the supernatant was retained. AChE activity was determined using a commercial kit (No.BC2025, Solarbio), and absorbance was measured at 412 nm using an enzyme marker. All procedures were performed according to the manufacturer's instructions.

The brain tissue was analyzed after homogenization to measure the levels of malondialdehyde (MDA, No. BC0025, Solarbio), superoxide dismutase (SOD, No. BC0175, Solarbio, China), and glutathione peroxidase (GSH-Px, BC1175, Solarbio) activity to determine the level of oxidative stress in the brain tissue. The kits were purchased from Nanjing Jiancheng. All procedures were performed in strict accordance with the manufacturer's instructions.

After centrifugation of the serum at 4 °C for 10 min at a rate of 12,000 rpm, 400 µL of methanol was added. The mixture was vortexed for one min and sonicated for 10 min in ice water. Samples were kept at -20 °C for 1 h to allow complete precipitation of proteins and other macromolecules. The supernatant was collected after centrifugation at 12,000 rpm for 15 min, transferred to a high-performance liquid chromatography vial, and analyzed using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS).

After treatment, the intestinal contents of the hindgut were carefully isolated in a sterile environment. Genomic DNA was extracted from intestinal contents using a DNA kit (TIANGEN Biotechnology Ltd., Beijing, China). After DNA extraction, the concentration and purity of the DNA were assessed using agarose gel electrophoresis, followed by gel recovery for DNA purification. After purification, PCR amplification of bacterial 16S rRNA (V3-V4 region) was performed using bacterial universal primer 338F/806R. Library construction and sequencing were performed at Majorbio Biomedical Technologies (Shanghai, China). In addition, sequencing data were analyzed by using Majorbio's analysis system (www.majorbio.com).

Statistical analysis of all data was performed using SPSS (version 23.0; Chicago, IL, USA) and graphing was performed using GraphPad Prism 8.0.2 (San Diego, CA, USA). Data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation (mean ± SD). The Shapiro-Wilk test was used to verify the normality of the data. One-way Analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used to compare the difference between the control and other groups, and Tukey's test was used to determine the significance (P < 0.05). The R package (R base package 3.5.0) was used to analyze the metabolomic data. Statistical analyses included univariate and multivariate analyses. Univariate statistical analyses included Tukey's test and Student's t-test, whereas multivariate analyses included principal component analysis and orthogonal partial least squares discriminant analysis (OPLS-DA, MetaboAnalystR 1.0.1). The LC-MS data were annotated by comparing the Kyoto genes with the Genome Encyclopedia database.

The uptake and bioaccumulation of CQDs in the brain are shown in fluorograms (Fig. 1 and S1). No fluorescence was observed in the brains of control carp throughout the experiment. Blue fluorescence was observed in the brain tissue of the CQDs group, indicating CQDs accumulation in the brain tissue. The intensity of fluorescence in brain tissue decreased after probiotic treatment.

To investigate the effects of CQDs exposure and probiotic treatment on brain histomorphology, HE staining was performed on the brain sections (Fig. 2 ). The histomorphometric analysis revealed a higher number of microglia in the CQDs-exposed as group compared to the control group (Fig. S2). Additionally, some neurons showed atrophy, indicating that CQDs exposure had a pathological impact on the carp's brain tissue. However, the brain tissue damage was mitigated after treatment with probiotics.

Fig. 3 illustrates the alterations in biochemical parameters in the brains of each experimental group after CQDs exposure and probiotic treatment. In comparison to the control group, CQDs exposure caused a decrease in the activity of SOD and GSH-Px, as well as an increase in the content of MDA, indicating a significant decrease in total antioxidant levels. Furthermore, CQDs exposure influenced AChE activity (Fig. 3). AChE activity was significantly decreased after CQDs exposure and increased after probiotic treatment in comparison to controls.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Expression of autophagy-, inflammation-, immunity-, tight junction-, and oxidative stress-related genes in the brain
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 shows the expression levels of autophagy-, inflammation-, immunity-, and tight junction-related genes in the brain of each experimental group after CQDs exposure and probiotic treatment. Two genes related to autophagy “atg5 and lc3-II” showed significantly higher expression levels after CQDs exposure, and autophagy levels decreased after probiotic recovery (Fig. 4A). In addition, the expression levels of apoptosis-related factors caspase-3 and caspase-9 increased after CQDs exposure, while the apoptosis inhibitory factor bcl2 significantly decreased (Fig. 4A). The expression levels of these factors approached those of the control group after probiotic treatment. To investigate the effects of CQDs exposure and probiotic treatment on brain tissue integrity, we examined the expression levels of tight junction genes in the brain (Fig. 4B). The results showed that CQDs exposure resulted in a significant decrease in the gene expression levels of occludin, claudin-2, and zonula occludens-1 and an increase in these expression levels after probiotic recovery. Exposure to CQDs significantly increased the expression of pro-inflammatory factors IL-1β and TNF-α and significantly decreased the expression levels of anti-inflammatory factor IL-10, antioxidant-related factors nrf2 and ho-1, and complement c3. The expression of these genes returned to control levels after probiotic treatment ( Fig. 4C).

After measuring and analyzing the swimming acceleration, speed, total distance, and exploration rate of each carp group, we found that CQDs exposure had an inhibitory effect on the locomotor behavior of carp (Fig. 5 ). CQDs exposure significantly decreased the swimming speed and acceleration of carp, whereas the probiotic treatment increased the swimming acceleration, speed, total distance, and exploration rate of carp. In addition, the probiotic treatment-alone group also experienced increased swimming acceleration, velocity, total distance, and exploration rates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Diversity and composition of gut microbiota
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the effects of CQDs exposure and probiotic treatment on gut flora, we assessed the composition and diversity of the gut flora using 16S rRNA analysis. Dilution curve smoothing indicated sufficient sequencing depth (Fig. 6 C). In this study, the Chao and Shannon indices were calculated to reflect the diversity and richness of the gut microbial community in each group. As shown in Fig. 6A-B, the Chao1 and Shannon indices of the CQDs group were significantly lower than those of the control and probiotic-treated groups. The β-diversity of the gut microorganisms was assessed using principal coordinate analysis (PCoA) (Fig. 6D). The PCoA results showed that the microorganisms in each experimental group were widely distributed and differed significantly among groups, indicating that CQDs exposure and probiotic treatment affected the β-diversity of gut microorganisms in carp. Further analysis of the composition of intestinal microorganisms revealed Proteobacteria, Actinobacteria, Fusobacteria, Bacteroidetes, and Firmicutes as the major phyla (Fig. 6E). The abundance of Firmicutes, Bacteroidetes, and Verrucomicrobiota decreased, and the abundance of Fusobacteria and Actinobacteria increased after CQDs exposure compared to that of the control group. The abundance of Firmicutes and Verrucomicrobiota increased after probiotic treatment compared to that in the CQDs group.

To further assess the differences in the gut microbiota among the groups, we conducted an LDA effect size (LEfSe) analysis (Fig. S3). The results revealed that the abundance of Mycobacterium and Nakamurella was higher in the CQDs group compared to the control group, while the abundance of Bacteroides and Cetobacterium was lower. Moreover, after probiotic treatment, the abundance of Mycobacterium was reduced, and that of Rhodobacter was significantly increased compared to the levels in the CQDs group.

In the OPLS-DA model, the values for R2Y and Q2 were 0.998 and 0.584, respectively, and P (CV-ANOVA) < 0.005, indicating a strong predictive potential and a good fit of the model (Fig. S4A). The fractional OPLS-DA plot demonstrated the distribution of metabolites in each group (Fig. S4B–D). The metabolites in the control group clustered together and were clearly separated from those in the CQDs group. The probiotic recovery group was distinct from the CQDs treatment group; however, some metabolites overlapped between the probiotic recovery and control groups.

We selected a subset of metabolites that differed to create a heat map (Fig. S4E). The levels of N-desmethyltramadol, 2-hydroxydiazepam, leukotriene E4, sufentanil, and brefeldin A were upregulated in the CQDs group compared to those in the control group, while the levels of spermine were downregulated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Correlation analysis of differential metabolites with apoptosis, oxidative stress, inflammation, and behavior
ce:section.ce:para: Spearman's correlation analysis was used to assess the potential relationship between metabolites and related indicators, such as inflammation, apoptosis, oxidative stress, and behavior. As shown in Fig. 7 A, the levels of proxyphene, N-desmethyltramadol, 2-hydroxydesipramine, leukotriene E4, sufentanyl, brefeldin A, dioctyl pathalate, and apricindine significantly increased and decreased following CQDs exposure and probiotic treatment, respectively. In addition, spermidine levels decreased after CQDs exposure and returned to the control level after probiotic treatment. Fig. 7B shows the correlation analysis of these metabolites with apoptosis, oxidative stress, inflammation, and behavior-related indices. Propropoxyphene, N-desmethyltramadol, 2-hydroxydesipramine, leukotriene E4, sufentanyl, brefeldin A, dioctyl pathalate, and aprindine were negatively correlated with total distance, swimming speed, mean speed, exploration speed, and bcl-2 and positively correlated with caspase-3 and caspase-9. In addition, spermidine was positively correlated with SOD, GSH-Px, and IL-10 and negatively correlated with IL-1β and TNF-α.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spearman correlation analysis of intestinal microorganisms and serum metabolites
ce:section.ce:para: We conducted Spearman's correlation analysis at the genus level to evaluate the relationship between the top 30 differential flora and selected differential serum metabolites (Fig. 8 ). Aprindine, N-desmethytramadol, sufentanyl, leukotriene E4, and dioctyl phthalate were positively correlated with Reyranella, Mycobacterium, and Xanthobacteraceae and negatively correlated with Rhodococcus, whereas, spermidine was negatively correlated with Reyranella, Mycobacterium, and Xanthobacteraceae. Finally, brefeldin A was positively correlated with Roseomonas, Kaistia, Aeromonas, and Flavobacterium.

Nanomaterials can accumulate in the brains of aquatic organisms, leading to morphological changes in neurons and nuclei and inducing neurotoxicity (Cho et al., 2022). Studies have shown that the administration of probiotics has a positive effect on nerve damage (Panther et al., 2022). For instance, lactobacillus has been shown to play a neuroprotective role in brain-injured mice via the gut-brain axis (Ma et al., 2019). In the present study, CQDs accumulated in brain tissue, suggesting that CQDs can cross the BBB. The tight junctions between endothelial cells in the BBB form a strong physical barrier, and their impairment is the basis of BBB dysfunction in various neurodegenerative diseases (Sasson et al., 2021). Tight junctions are composed of tight junction proteins such as occludin, claudin, and zonula occludens-1 (zo-1). Studies have also shown that Lactobacillus rhamnosus, a probiotic, can restore the decrease mRNA expression of ZO-1, claudin-1, and occludin caused by E.coli infection (Bhat et al., 2020). Consistent with the findings of this study, probiotics were able to ameliorate the reduction of zo-1, claudin-1 and occludin mRNA expression caused by CQDs, suggesting that probiotics can improve the physical brain barrier damage caused by CQDs. Furthermore, the histomorphometric analysis revealed that exposure to CQDs resulted in brain damage, as evidenced by pathological features such as increase in the number of microglia and neuronal atrophy. However, the damage was relatively mild in the probiotic recovery group. Therefore, it is hypothesized that probiotic supplementation can effectively alleviate CQD-induced neurotoxicity in carp.

AChE is a key neurotransmitter in both the central and peripheral nervous systems, and AChE inhibition is an indicator of neurotoxicity (Attig et al., 2010). Studies have shown that exposure to nanomaterials decreases the AChE activity of Scrobicularia plana (Ribeiro et al., 2017). In contrast, Bacillus licheniformis increased the activity of AChE (Gopi et al., 2022). Consistent with these findings, we observed a decrease in the activity of AChE induced by CQDs and an increase in its activity following probiotic supplementation. These results further confirmed that CQDs induced neurotoxicity in carp, and probiotic supplementation effectively alleviated it.

Exposure to carbon nanomaterials has been reported to cause oxidative stress in zebrafish, leading to tissue damage (Souza et al., 2017). MDA is a biomarker of lipid peroxidation and oxidative stress, whereas GSH-Px and SOD are indicators of antioxidant activity. Furthermore, HO-1 is an important intracellular antioxidant stress pathway that is regulated by the transcription factor Nrf2 at the transcriptional level and plays an anti-oxidative stress and anti-inflammatory role (Chen et al., 2015). In this study, probiotic treatment increased antioxidant levels and reduced oxidative stress. Consistent with the results of this study, Lactobacillus (a type of probiotic) has antioxidant activity that modulates oxidative stress and protects cells from oxidative damage (Lin et al., 2022), suggesting that enhancing antioxidant capacity and reducing oxidative stress may be one of the ways in which probiotics can alleviate CQD-induced brain damage.

Abnormal neuroinflammation, autophagy, and apoptosis are prerequisite factors for neuronal cell damage (Wang et al., 2020). Quantum dots can induce autophagy in different cell lines and organs (Chen et al., 2013), and CQDs can trigger apoptosis in osteosarcoma 143 B cells through the mitochondrial apoptosis signaling pathway (Jiao et al., 2020). Therefore, we measured the gene expression levels of pro-inflammatory factors, anti-inflammatory factors, and autophagy and apoptosis-related factors after CQDs exposure and probiotic treatment. The results showed that probiotics could reduce the elevated levels of inflammation, apoptosis and autophagy caused by CQDs. These findings are consistent with previous studies, which have confirmed that probiotics can improve immune function and reduce inflammation and cell apoptosis by modulating intestinal microbiota (Wu et al., 2019). Based on these results, we hypothesize that probiotics may alleviate nerve damage by reducing levels of inflammation, autophagy and apoptosis.

Nervous system damage leads to behavioral change. Previous research has shown that mice with brain damage exhibit reduced swimming and exploration behaviors (Rowe et al., 2016). Similarly, studies have demonstrated that nanomaterials can alter various behaviors in zebrafish, including social interaction, average speed, and exploration, which can disturb their swimming patterns (Li et al., 2017). In this study, we evaluated the effects of CQDs exposure and probiotic treatment on the free-swimming behavior of carp by measuring the total swimming distance, speed, average speed, and exploration rate. The results of our behavioral tests showed that probiotic treatment alleviated the decrease in the swimming performance of carp caused by CQDs exposure. Moreover, probiotic treatment has been reported to improve exploration rate and attention in zebrafish, which is consistent with our findings (Borrelli et al., 2016).

Gut microbial composition and abundance and their associated metabolites, which include neurotransmitters and their precursors, play an important role in the normal functional functioning of the brain's nervous system. Studies have shown that some flora can be involved in the production of some neurotransmitters related to nervous system diseases, for example, Escherichia coli, Bacteroides, Eubacterium and Bifidobacterium (Chen et al., 2021). Mycobacterium contains a large number of pathogenic bacteria, which can cause infections in aquatic organisms, decrease immunity, and change behavior (Delghandi et al., 2020). Spores produced by Firmicutes can resist dehydration and extreme environments and are related to the immune function of fish (Dehler et al., 2017). In addtion, firmicutes also protect the intestinal barrier; a decrease in their abundance will damage the intestinal barrier, and the level of inflammation of organisms will increase (Huang et al., 2018). Additionally, a significant correlation has been observed between the abundance of Firmicutes and behavioral changes (Huang et al., 2018). In this study, CQDs exposure led to a decline in fish immune function, which may be related to the reduction in Firmicutes. Furthermore, we observed an increase in the α-diversity of intestinal microorganisms and the abundance of Verrucomicrobia after the administration of probiotics. This finding suggests that probiotics may alleviate brain damage and behavioral changes by improving intestinal microecological disturbances caused by CQDs.

Previous studies have shown that probiotic intervention can alleviate metabolic disorders caused by toxic substances (Zhang et al., 2021) and perfluorobutanesulfonate exposure (Hu et al., 2021). Consistent with these findings, our study showed that probiotics restored some of the metabolites altered by CQDs exposure to normal levels. Leukotriene is a lipid mediator that promotes inflammation responses and arouses adaptive immune responses (Peters-Golden et al., 2005). Brefeldin A was shown to induce apoptosis and inhibit the growth of various human cell lines (Pommepuy et al., 2003). In the present study, the levels of Leukotriene E4 and Brefeldin A increased after CQDs treatment, consistent with the increased expression levels of inflammatory and apoptosis-related genes after CQDs treatment. Furthermore, sufentanyl can exert analgesic effects through the nervous system (Baehr et al., 2022). Spermidine can penetrate the BBB of mice and increase hyperphosphorylation and mitochondrial function of the hippocampal eIF5A (Schroeder et al., 2021). In this study, sufentanyl was negatively correlated with the total distance, swimming speed, average acceleration, and exploration rate, whereas spermidine was positively correlated with these behavior-related indicators. This suggests that probiotics have the potential to mitigate changes in carp behavior induced by CQDs exposure by affecting neurological or mitochondrial function.

It was confirmed that alterations in serum metabolome profiles can reflect differences in the gut microbiome of animals, which is consistent with our findings (Huang et al., 2019). The serum metabolites aprindine, N-desmethytramadol, sufentanyl, leukotriene E4, and dioctyl phthalate in this study were positively associated with Reyranella, Mycobacterium, and Xanthobacteraceae and negatively associated with Rhodococcus. Mycobacterium can cause biological infections by forming biofilms (Delghandi et al., 2020), which leads to an inflammatory response in the body, and leukotriene E4 has also been shown to be associated with inflammation. This suggests that Mycobacterium and leukotriene E4 may be linked in some way, but the exact mechanism remains to be studied.

The health of the gut microbiome is crucial in regulating overall organism function and health (Panther et al., 2022). Gut flora has been reported to be a therapeutic target for brain injury and behavioral changes (Rice et al., 2019). The present study demonstrated that CQDs exposure leads to disruption of the gut microbiome and the blood-brain barrier. According to a study, the brain and the gut barrier are highly correlated, and the gut barrier is influenced by the composition of the gut microbiota (Chen et al., 2019). This suggests that CQDs may first cause gut microbial disruption, which then leads to gut-brain barrier damage, thus allowing CQDs to accumulate in the brain and cause brain damage. More and more evidence shows that microbiota can produce and release some cytokines and other active substances through the blood-brain barrier to regulate many functions of the central nervous system (CNS) (Logsdon et al., 2017). It has been reported that metabolites play an important role as mediators between the brain and the gut microbiota (Wang et al., 2021). This suggests that CQDs may be altered by altering the gut microbiota and thus the levels of some metabolites, which are then transmitted to brain tissue through the systemic circulation to cause brain damage and behavioral changes. Probiotics can improve the intestinal microbial composition, potentially reducing the damage to the brain barrier and alleviating the brain tissue damage. This suggests that probiotics act through the gut-brain axis to ameliorate the neurotoxicity caused by CQDs.

In conclusion, this study found that CQDs, due to their small particle size, easily penetrate the BBB and accumulate in the brain, causing damage to the tight junctions of brain tissue (direct pathway), oxidative stress, an inflammatory response, and brain damage. Oxidative stress (indirect pathway) inhibits AChE and affects the behavior of carp. In addition, CQDs disrupt the microbial composition of the fish gut and alter the levels of some small-molecule metabolites associated with nerves (indirect pathway). These neural-related small molecules affect neurotransmission, further explaining behavioral changes. In contrast, probiotic supplementation ameliorated dysbiosis and metabolic disturbances in the intestinal flora caused by CQDs, alleviating neurotoxicity and behavioral abnormalities. This addition of probiotics may have reshaped the gut microbiota of the carp. However, the direct and indirect interactions between CQDs and gut microbes deserve further investigation. This study provides basic data on the biosafety of CQDs in addition to providing a reference for exploring methods to mitigate the toxicity of CQDs.

Xianglin Cao: Conceptualization, Methodology. Rongjie Yuan: Methodology. Dandan Sun: Methodology. Xinyu Ji: Software. Yinyin Wei: Software. Lulu Li: Data curation. Suqi Guo: Data curation. Baohua Li: Visualization, Validation. Jianjun Chen: Project administration, Conceptualization, Resources, Data curation, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Methodology, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Henan Province Science and Technology Development Plan Project in China (grant number 212102110359, 222102110362).
This work was supported by the Innovation Project of Henan Normal University (YL202004).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.aquatox.2023.106508.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yamanaka O., & Takeuchi R. 2018. UMATracker: an intuitive image-based tracking platform. J. Exp. Biol., 221(16). 10.1242/jeb.182469

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhang et al., 2021

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Alleviation of neuronal cell death and memory deficit with chungkookjang made with bacillus amyloliquefaciens and bacillus subtilis potentially through promoting gut–brain axis in artery-occluded gerbils

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang T., Ryu M.S., Wu X., Yang H.J., Jeong S.J., Seo J.W., Jeong D.Y., & Park S. 2021. Alleviation of neuronal cell death and memory deficit with chungkookjang made with bacillus amyloliquefaciens and bacillus subtilis potentially through promoting gut–brain axis in artery-occluded gerbils. Foods, 10(11): 2697. 10.3390/foods10112697

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Perturbation of physiological systems by nanoparticles
